methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $17M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Floodbase is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Bessie Schwarz and Dr. Beth Tellman, having raised $17M to date. The broader Climate & Energy sector includes one public comparable (LNZA) and eight private peers, including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen. However, the peer set does not yield sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation tiers to support a reliable peer-median approach.

Valuation analysis therefore relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to later-stage transitions. Using the $17M total raised as a proxy for current enterprise value, this method implies a post-raise valuation of $34M. The narrow methodological foundation—one applicable approach rather than triangulation across multiple methods—limits confidence in precision.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $29M to $39M, with a median point of $34M. The range reflects standard variance around the stage-progression point estimate rather than cross-method convergence. Key uncertainties include the absence of tight peer comparables, which prevents benchmarking against similar-stage climate-tech companies, and reliance on raised capital as a proxy for current value. Investors should view this range as indicative of order of magnitude pending availability of stronger comparable data or income-based methods.
